question:
7. Let \( a \) and \( b \) be relatively prime positive integers, \( b \) even. For each positive integer \( q \) let \( p=p(q) \) be chosen so that
\[
\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|
\]
is a minimum. Prove that
\[
\lim _{n-\infty} \sum_{y=1}^{n} \frac{q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|}{n}=\frac{1}{4} .
\]
solution:
Solution. Rewrite \( q|p / q-a / b| \) in the form
\[
\frac{1}{b}|p b-q a| .
\]

For each \( q \) we are to choose \( p \) to minimize this; then \( p b \) - \( q a \) will be the absolutely least residue of \( q a \) modulo \( b \). Since \( a \) is relatively prime to \( b \), as \( q \) varies through a complete set of residues modulo \( b \), so will \( q a \), and therefore \( p b-q a \) will take the values
\[
-C+1,-C+2, \ldots-1,0,1, \ldots, C-1, C
\]
where \( b=2 C \) (recall that \( b \) is even) and the contribution to the sum will be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{b}(0+1+2+\cdots+ & C-1+C+C-1+\cdots+1) \\
& =\frac{C^{2}}{2 C}=b / 4
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus if \( n=b \cdot r+s \) where \( 0 \leq s<b \), we have
\[
\sum_{q=1}^{n} q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|=r \frac{b}{4}+\sum_{q=b r+1}^{b r+s} q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|
\]
since \( q \) runs through \( r \) complete residue systems \( (\bmod b) \) and then the integers \( b r+1, b r+2, \ldots, b r+s \). Since \( p(q) \) is the integer nearest to \( q a / b \),
\[
q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|=\left|p-\frac{q a}{b}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}
\]

We have therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left|\frac{1}{4} n-\sum_{q=1}^{n} q\right| \frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}| | & =\left|\frac{s}{4}-\sum_{q=b r+1}^{b r+s} q\right| \frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}| | . \\
& \leq \frac{s}{4}+s \sup _{q} q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right| \\
& \leq \frac{3}{4} s<\frac{3}{4} b .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( n \), we get
\[
\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{q=1}^{n} q\right| \frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}| |<\frac{3 b}{4 n} .
\]

Letting \( n \rightarrow \infty \), we see that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{q=1}^{n} q\left|\frac{p}{q}-\frac{a}{b}\right|=\frac{1}{4} .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
7. Let \( numerrel \) and \( evenrel \) be relatively prime positive integers, \( evenrel \) even. For each positive integer \( denomiter \) let \( nearintg=nearintg(denomiter) \) be chosen so that
\[
\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|
\]
is a minimum. Prove that
\[
\lim _{termlimit-\infty} \sum_{sumindex=1}^{termlimit} \frac{denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|}{termlimit}=\frac{1}{4} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Rewrite \( denomiter|nearintg / denomiter-numerrel / evenrel| \) in the form
\[
\frac{1}{evenrel}|nearintg\,evenrel-denomiter\,numerrel| .
\]

For each \( denomiter \) we are to choose \( nearintg \) to minimize this; then \( nearintg\,evenrel-denomiter\,numerrel \) will be the absolutely least residue of \( denomiter\,numerrel \) modulo \( evenrel \). Since \( numerrel \) is relatively prime to \( evenrel \), as \( denomiter \) varies through a complete set of residues modulo \( evenrel \), so will \( denomiter\,numerrel \), and therefore \( nearintg\,evenrel-denomiter\,numerrel \) will take the values
\[
-halfdeno+1,-halfdeno+2, \ldots-1,0,1, \ldots, halfdeno-1, halfdeno
\]
where \( evenrel=2\,halfdeno \) (recall that \( evenrel \) is even) and the contribution to the sum will be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{evenrel}(0+1+2+\cdots+ & halfdeno-1+halfdeno+halfdeno-1+\cdots+1) \\
& =\frac{halfdeno^{2}}{2\,halfdeno}=evenrel / 4
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus if \( termlimit=evenrel \cdot quotcount+remablock \) where \( 0 \leq remablock<evenrel \), we have
\[
\sum_{denomiter=1}^{termlimit} denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|=quotcount \frac{evenrel}{4}+\sum_{denomiter=evenrel\,quotcount+1}^{evenrel\,quotcount+remablock} denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|
\]
since \( denomiter \) runs through \( quotcount \) complete residue systems \((\bmod evenrel)\) and then the integers \( evenrel\,quotcount+1, evenrel\,quotcount+2, \ldots, evenrel\,quotcount+remablock \). Since \( nearintg(denomiter) \) is the integer nearest to \( denomiter\,numerrel / evenrel \),
\[
denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|=\left|nearintg-\frac{denomiter\,numerrel}{evenrel}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}
\]

We have therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left|\frac{1}{4} termlimit-\sum_{denomiter=1}^{termlimit} denomiter\right| \frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}| | & =\left|\frac{remablock}{4}-\sum_{denomiter=evenrel\,quotcount+1}^{evenrel\,quotcount+remablock} denomiter\right| \frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}| | . \\
& \leq \frac{remablock}{4}+remablock \sup _{denomiter} denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right| \\
& \leq \frac{3}{4} remablock<\frac{3}{4} evenrel .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( termlimit \), we get
\[
\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{termlimit} \sum_{denomiter=1}^{termlimit} denomiter\right| \frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}| |<\frac{3\,evenrel}{4\,termlimit} .
\]

Letting \( termlimit \rightarrow \infty \), we see that
\[
\lim _{termlimit \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{termlimit} \sum_{denomiter=1}^{termlimit} denomiter\left|\frac{nearintg}{denomiter}-\frac{numerrel}{evenrel}\right|=\frac{1}{4} .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. Let \( riverbank \) and \( thunderous \) be relatively prime positive integers, \( thunderous \) even. For each positive integer \( shoreline \) let \( tapestry=tapestry(shoreline) \) be chosen so that
\[
\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|
\]
is a minimum. Prove that
\[
\lim _{hazelnuts-\infty} \sum_{corridor=1}^{hazelnuts} \frac{shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|}{hazelnuts}=\frac{1}{4} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Rewrite \( shoreline|tapestry / shoreline-riverbank / thunderous| \) in the form
\[
\frac{1}{thunderous}|tapestry\, thunderous-\, shoreline\, riverbank| .
\]

For each \( shoreline \) we are to choose \( tapestry \) to minimize this; then \( tapestry\, thunderous-\, shoreline\, riverbank \) will be the absolutely least residue of \( shoreline\, riverbank \) modulo \( thunderous \). Since \( riverbank \) is relatively prime to \( thunderous \), as \( shoreline \) varies through a complete set of residues modulo \( thunderous \), so will \( shoreline\, riverbank \), and therefore \( tapestry\, thunderous-shoreline\, riverbank \) will take the values
\[
-monument+1,-monument+2, \ldots-1,0,1, \ldots, monument-1, monument
\]
where \( thunderous=2 monument \) (recall that \( thunderous \) is even) and the contribution to the sum will be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{thunderous}(0+1+2+\cdots+ & monument-1+monument+monument-1+\cdots+1) \\
& =\frac{monument^{2}}{2 monument}=thunderous / 4
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus if \( hazelnuts=thunderous \cdot gemstone+lighthouse \) where \( 0 \leq lighthouse<thunderous \), we have
\[
\sum_{shoreline=1}^{hazelnuts} shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|=gemstone \frac{thunderous}{4}+\sum_{shoreline=thunderous\, gemstone+1}^{thunderous\, gemstone+lighthouse} shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|
\]
since \( shoreline \) runs through \( gemstone \) complete residue systems \( (\bmod thunderous) \) and then the integers \( thunderous\, gemstone+1, thunderous\, gemstone+2, \ldots, thunderous\, gemstone+lighthouse \). Since \( tapestry(shoreline) \) is the integer nearest to \( \dfrac{shoreline\, riverbank}{thunderous} \),
\[
shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|=\left|tapestry-\frac{shoreline\, riverbank}{thunderous}\right| \le \frac{1}{2}
\]

We have therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left|\frac{1}{4}\, hazelnuts-\sum_{shoreline=1}^{hazelnuts} shoreline\right|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}|\,| & =\left|\frac{lighthouse}{4}-\sum_{shoreline=thunderous\, gemstone+1}^{thunderous\, gemstone+lighthouse} shoreline\right|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}|\,| \\
& \le \frac{lighthouse}{4}+lighthouse\, \sup_{shoreline} shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right| \\
& \le \frac{3}{4}\, lighthouse<\frac{3}{4}\, thunderous .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( hazelnuts \), we get
\[
\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{hazelnuts}\sum_{shoreline=1}^{hazelnuts} shoreline\right|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}|\,|<\frac{3\, thunderous}{4\, hazelnuts} .
\]

Letting \( hazelnuts \rightarrow \infty \), we see that
\[
\lim_{hazelnuts \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{hazelnuts} \sum_{shoreline=1}^{hazelnuts} shoreline\left|\frac{tapestry}{shoreline}-\frac{riverbank}{thunderous}\right|=\frac{1}{4} .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Let \( composite \) and \( oddnumber \) be relatively prime positive integers, \( oddnumber \) even. For each positive integer \( constantval \) let \( randomized=randomized(constantval) \) be chosen so that
\[
\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|
\]
is a minimum. Prove that
\[
\lim _{fractional-\infty} \sum_{fixedpoint=1}^{fractional} \frac{constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|}{fractional}=\frac{1}{4} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Rewrite \( constantval|randomized / constantval-composite / oddnumber| \) in the form
\[
\frac{1}{oddnumber}|randomized \, oddnumber-constantval \, composite| .
\]

For each \( constantval \) we are to choose \( randomized \) to minimize this; then \( randomized \, oddnumber \) \- \( constantval \, composite \) will be the absolutely least residue of \( constantval \, composite \) modulo \( oddnumber \). Since \( composite \) is relatively prime to \( oddnumber \), as \( constantval \) varies through a complete set of residues modulo \( oddnumber \), so will \( constantval \, composite \), and therefore \( randomized \, oddnumber-constantval \, composite \) will take the values
\[
-doubleval+1,-doubleval+2, \ldots-1,0,1, \ldots, doubleval-1, doubleval
\]
where \( oddnumber=2 \, doubleval \) (recall that \( oddnumber \) is even) and the contribution to the sum will be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{oddnumber}(0+1+2+\cdots+ & doubleval-1+doubleval+doubleval-1+\cdots+1) \\
& =\frac{doubleval^{2}}{2 \, doubleval}=oddnumber / 4
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus if \( fractional=oddnumber \cdot incomplete+quotientval \) where \( 0 \leq quotientval<oddnumber \), we have
\[
\sum_{constantval=1}^{fractional} constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|=incomplete \frac{oddnumber}{4}+\sum_{constantval=oddnumber \, incomplete+1}^{oddnumber \, incomplete+quotientval} constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|
\]
since \( constantval \) runs through \( incomplete \) complete residue systems \( (\bmod oddnumber) \) and then the integers \( oddnumber \, incomplete+1, oddnumber \, incomplete+2, \ldots, oddnumber \, incomplete+quotientval \). Since \( randomized(constantval) \) is the integer nearest to \( constantval \, composite / oddnumber \),
\[
constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|=\left|randomized-\frac{constantval \, composite}{oddnumber}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}
\]

We have therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left|\frac{1}{4} \, fractional-\sum_{constantval=1}^{fractional} constantval\right| \frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}| | & =\left|\frac{quotientval}{4}-\sum_{constantval=oddnumber \, incomplete+1}^{oddnumber \, incomplete+quotientval} constantval\right| \frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}| | . \\
& \leq \frac{quotientval}{4}+quotientval \sup _{constantval} constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right| \\
& \leq \frac{3}{4} \, quotientval<\frac{3}{4} \, oddnumber .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( fractional \), we get
\[
\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{fractional} \sum_{constantval=1}^{fractional} constantval\right| \frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}| |<\frac{3 \, oddnumber}{4 \, fractional} .
\]

Letting \( fractional \rightarrow \infty \), we see that
\[
\lim _{fractional \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{fractional} \sum_{constantval=1}^{fractional} constantval\left|\frac{randomized}{constantval}-\frac{composite}{oddnumber}\right|=\frac{1}{4} .
\]

garbled_string.question:
7. Let \( dzmrfyga \) and \( glpksewn \) be relatively prime positive integers, \( glpksewn \) even. For each positive integer \( xjzfkrla \) let \( qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp(xjzfkrla) \) be chosen so that
\[
\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|
\]
is a minimum. Prove that
\[
\lim _{hjgrksla-\infty} \sum_{fbncqzlo=1}^{hjgrksla} \frac{xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|}{hjgrksla}=\frac{1}{4} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Rewrite \( xjzfkrla|qzxwvtnp / xjzfkrla-dzmrfyga / glpksewn| \) in the form
\[
\frac{1}{glpksewn}|qzxwvtnp glpksewn-xjzfkrla dzmrfyga| .
\]

For each \( xjzfkrla \) we are to choose \( qzxwvtnp \) to minimize this; then \( qzxwvtnp glpksewn \) - \( xjzfkrla dzmrfyga \) will be the absolutely least residue of \( xjzfkrla dzmrfyga \) modulo \( glpksewn \). Since \( dzmrfyga \) is relatively prime to \( glpksewn \), as \( xjzfkrla \) varies through a complete set of residues modulo \( glpksewn \), so will \( xjzfkrla dzmrfyga \), and therefore \( qzxwvtnp glpksewn-xjzfkrla dzmrfyga \) will take the values
\[
-ytrmnbav+1,-ytrmnbav+2, \ldots-1,0,1, \ldots, ytrmnbav-1, ytrmnbav
\]
where \( glpksewn=2 ytrmnbav \) (recall that \( glpksewn \) is even) and the contribution to the sum will be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{glpksewn}(0+1+2+\cdots+ & ytrmnbav-1+ytrmnbav+ytrmnbav-1+\cdots+1) \\
& =\frac{ytrmnbav^{2}}{2 ytrmnbav}=glpksewn / 4
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus if \( hjgrksla=glpksewn \cdot tmskldpj+vqnhdcej \) where \( 0 \leq vqnhdcej<glpksewn \), we have
\[
\sum_{xjzfkrla=1}^{hjgrksla} xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|=tmskldpj \frac{glpksewn}{4}+\sum_{xjzfkrla=glpksewn tmskldpj+1}^{glpksewn tmskldpj+vqnhdcej} xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|
\]
since \( xjzfkrla \) runs through \( tmskldpj \) complete residue systems \( (\bmod glpksewn) \) and then the integers \( glpksewn tmskldpj+1, glpksewn tmskldpj+2, \ldots, glpksewn tmskldpj+vqnhdcej \). Since \( qzxwvtnp(xjzfkrla) \) is the integer nearest to \( xjzfkrla dzmrfyga / glpksewn \),
\[
xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|=\left|qzxwvtnp-\frac{xjzfkrla dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}
\]

We have therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left|\frac{1}{4} hjgrksla-\sum_{xjzfkrla=1}^{hjgrksla} xjzfkrla\right| \frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}| | & =\left|\frac{vqnhdcej}{4}-\sum_{xjzfkrla=glpksewn tmskldpj+1}^{glpksewn tmskldpj+vqnhdcej} xjzfkrla\right| \frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}| | . \\
& \leq \frac{vqnhdcej}{4}+vqnhdcej \sup _{xjzfkrla} xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right| \\
& \leq \frac{3}{4} vqnhdcej<\frac{3}{4} glpksewn .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( hjgrksla \), we get
\[
\left|\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{hjgrksla} \sum_{xjzfkrla=1}^{hjgrksla} xjzfkrla\right| \frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}| |<\frac{3 glpksewn}{4 hjgrksla} .
\]

Letting \( hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty \), we see that
\[
\lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \sum_{xjzfkrla=1}^{hjgrksla} xjzfkrla\left|\frac{qzxwvtnp}{xjzfkrla}-\frac{dzmrfyga}{glpksewn}\right|=\frac{1}{4} .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  2.  
Let  

  (a_1,b_1), \ldots  , (a_d ,b_d) \in  \mathbb{N}^2  

be d ordered pairs such that  
1. gcd(a_i , b_i ) = 1 for every i,  
2. every b_i is odd, and  
3. the denominators are pair-wise coprime: gcd(b_i , b_j ) = 1 whenever i \neq  j.

For every integer q \geq  1 choose the integer vector  

  p(q) = (p_1(q), \ldots  , p_d(q)) \in  \mathbb{Z}^d  

by the ``nearest-integer'' rule  

  p_i(q) is the (unique) integer that minimises  
  | p / q - a_i / b_i |.  
If two integers are at the same distance we agree to take the larger one; this tie-breaking convention will never be needed except when b_i divides q a_i /2 and guarantees that p_i(q) is a well-defined function of q.

For every q define  

  F(q) = q^{\,d}\;\prod _{i=1}^{d} | p_i(q)/q - a_i/b_i |.

Put  

  B = lcm(b_1,\ldots ,b_d) = b_1\cdot \ldots \cdot b_d                         (because the b_i are pair-wise coprime).

Prove that the Cesaro mean of F taken over any block of n consecutive integers that starts immediately after a complete B-cycle converges, namely  

  lim_{n\to \infty }  1/(n+1) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q)  =  \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2).

Thus, no matter how large a block one takes, as long as it begins with B + 1 its average value stabilises to the constant on the right.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

  \delta _i(q) = | p_i(q) b_i - q a_i |            (0 \leq  \delta _i(q) \leq  (b_i-1)/2).   (1)

Because p_i(q) is chosen by the nearest-integer rule, \delta _i(q) is the absolutely least residue of q a_i modulo b_i.

Step 1.  Re-express F(q).  
From (1) we have

  | p_i(q)/q - a_i/b_i | = \delta _i(q)/(q b_i),

whence

  F(q) = q^{d} \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i(q)/(q b_i)  
        = \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i(q)/b_i.                                  (2)

Consequently F depends only on the residues of q modulo the b_i and is therefore B-periodic:

  F(q+B) = F(q) for every q.                                       (3)

Step 2.  The distribution of the residue vector.  
The Chinese Remainder Theorem gives a bijection

  q (mod B) \mapsto  ( q a_1 (mod b_1), \ldots , q a_d (mod b_d) )

from \mathbb{Z}/B\mathbb{Z} onto the product \prod  \mathbb{Z}/b_i\mathbb{Z}.  Hence, as q runs through any complete set of B consecutive integers, the ordinary residue vector

  r(q) = ( r_1(q), \ldots , r_d(q) ) with r_i(q) \equiv  q a_i (mod b_i)

is uniformly distributed on that product and its coordinates are independent.

Passing from r_i(q) to the absolute residues \delta _i(q) is deterministic: for each i

  \delta _i(q) = min{ r_i(q), b_i - r_i(q) }.

Because exactly two ordinary residues reduce to the same positive absolute residue \pm r (1 \leq  r \leq  (b_i-1)/2) and only one residue reduces to 0, we get the precise distribution  

  P[ \delta _i(q)=0 ] = 1/b_i ,    
  P[ \delta _i(q)=r ] = 2/b_i    (1\leq r\leq (b_i-1)/2).                      (4)

Independence of the coordinates survives this projection, so the random variables (\delta _1(q), \ldots , \delta _d(q)) are still independent.

Step 3.  Expectation of one coordinate.  
Write b_i = 2C_i + 1.  Using (4)

  E[ \delta _i ] = (1/b_i)\cdot 0 + (2/b_i)\cdot (1 + 2 + \ldots  + C_i)  
          = (2/b_i)\cdot C_i(C_i+1)/2  
          = ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i).                                  (5)

Step 4.  Expectation of the product.  
Because the \delta _i are independent,

  E[ \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i ] = \prod _{i=1}^{d} E[ \delta _i ]  
                      = \prod _{i=1}^{d} ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i).          (6)

Step 5.  Mean value over one complete B-block.  
Combining (2) and (6),

  (1/B) \sum _{q=1}^{B} F(q) = \prod _{i=1}^{d} ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i^2).    (7)

Step 6.  Cesaro mean over an arbitrary long block post-B.  
Because of the periodicity (3),

  \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) = \sum _{q=1}^{n} F(q).                        (8)

Write n = K B + r with 0 \leq  r < B.  Split the sum in (8) into the first K complete periods plus a tail of length r:

  \sum _{q=1}^{n} F(q)  
  = K \sum _{q=1}^{B} F(q) + \sum _{q=KB+1}^{KB+r} F(q).                  (9)

The first term equals K B\cdot \prod _{i}(b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2) by (7).
For the tail we use |\delta _i(q)| \leq  (b_i-1)/2, so, from (2),

  0 \leq  F(q) \leq  \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i-1)/(2 b_i) := M.                   (10)

Therefore the tail is < M B  (because r < B).  Putting everything together and dividing by n+1 = K B + r + 1,

  |  (1/(n+1)) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) - \prod _{i}(b_i^2-1)/(4 b_i^2) |
      \leq   (M B + \prod _{i}(b_i^2-1)/(4 b_i^2)\cdot (B-1)) / (n+1)            (11)

which is O(1/(n+1)).  Letting n \to  \infty  forces the right-hand side to 0, yielding

  lim_{n\to \infty }  1/(n+1) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) = \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2). \square

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimension: the problem moves from a single rational approximation to simultaneous approximation in $d$ dimensions, introducing the necessity of analysing joint residue behaviour.

2.  Additional variables and coprimality conditions: one must track an arbitrary number of denominators $b_{1},\dots ,b_{d}$, prove pairwise independence, and manage the least-residue calculation in each coordinate.

3.  Deeper theoretical tools: the solution relies on the Chinese Remainder Theorem to establish *uniform distribution* and *statistical independence* of a multidimensional residue vector, concepts absent from the original problem.

4.  More sophisticated averaging: the summand now involves a product of $d$ small differences and a factor $q^{d}$, so cancellations that were automatic in one dimension must be proved via expectation factorisation.

5.  Error control in incomplete cycles: bounding the tail for *all* $d$ simultaneously requires sharper uniform bounds than the scalar case.

6.  Conceptual load: the solver must blend elementary number theory (least residues, CRT) with probabilistic language (expectations, independence) and asymptotic analysis, making the variant substantially more intricate and multi-layered than either the original or the existing kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  2.  
Let  

  (a_1,b_1), \ldots  , (a_d ,b_d) \in  \mathbb{N}^2  

be d ordered pairs such that  
1. gcd(a_i , b_i ) = 1 for every i,  
2. every b_i is odd, and  
3. the denominators are pair-wise coprime: gcd(b_i , b_j ) = 1 whenever i \neq  j.

For every integer q \geq  1 choose the integer vector  

  p(q) = (p_1(q), \ldots  , p_d(q)) \in  \mathbb{Z}^d  

by the ``nearest-integer'' rule  

  p_i(q) is the (unique) integer that minimises  
  | p / q - a_i / b_i |.  
If two integers are at the same distance we agree to take the larger one; this tie-breaking convention will never be needed except when b_i divides q a_i /2 and guarantees that p_i(q) is a well-defined function of q.

For every q define  

  F(q) = q^{\,d}\;\prod _{i=1}^{d} | p_i(q)/q - a_i/b_i |.

Put  

  B = lcm(b_1,\ldots ,b_d) = b_1\cdot \ldots \cdot b_d                         (because the b_i are pair-wise coprime).

Prove that the Cesaro mean of F taken over any block of n consecutive integers that starts immediately after a complete B-cycle converges, namely  

  lim_{n\to \infty }  1/(n+1) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q)  =  \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2).

Thus, no matter how large a block one takes, as long as it begins with B + 1 its average value stabilises to the constant on the right.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

  \delta _i(q) = | p_i(q) b_i - q a_i |            (0 \leq  \delta _i(q) \leq  (b_i-1)/2).   (1)

Because p_i(q) is chosen by the nearest-integer rule, \delta _i(q) is the absolutely least residue of q a_i modulo b_i.

Step 1.  Re-express F(q).  
From (1) we have

  | p_i(q)/q - a_i/b_i | = \delta _i(q)/(q b_i),

whence

  F(q) = q^{d} \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i(q)/(q b_i)  
        = \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i(q)/b_i.                                  (2)

Consequently F depends only on the residues of q modulo the b_i and is therefore B-periodic:

  F(q+B) = F(q) for every q.                                       (3)

Step 2.  The distribution of the residue vector.  
The Chinese Remainder Theorem gives a bijection

  q (mod B) \mapsto  ( q a_1 (mod b_1), \ldots , q a_d (mod b_d) )

from \mathbb{Z}/B\mathbb{Z} onto the product \prod  \mathbb{Z}/b_i\mathbb{Z}.  Hence, as q runs through any complete set of B consecutive integers, the ordinary residue vector

  r(q) = ( r_1(q), \ldots , r_d(q) ) with r_i(q) \equiv  q a_i (mod b_i)

is uniformly distributed on that product and its coordinates are independent.

Passing from r_i(q) to the absolute residues \delta _i(q) is deterministic: for each i

  \delta _i(q) = min{ r_i(q), b_i - r_i(q) }.

Because exactly two ordinary residues reduce to the same positive absolute residue \pm r (1 \leq  r \leq  (b_i-1)/2) and only one residue reduces to 0, we get the precise distribution  

  P[ \delta _i(q)=0 ] = 1/b_i ,    
  P[ \delta _i(q)=r ] = 2/b_i    (1\leq r\leq (b_i-1)/2).                      (4)

Independence of the coordinates survives this projection, so the random variables (\delta _1(q), \ldots , \delta _d(q)) are still independent.

Step 3.  Expectation of one coordinate.  
Write b_i = 2C_i + 1.  Using (4)

  E[ \delta _i ] = (1/b_i)\cdot 0 + (2/b_i)\cdot (1 + 2 + \ldots  + C_i)  
          = (2/b_i)\cdot C_i(C_i+1)/2  
          = ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i).                                  (5)

Step 4.  Expectation of the product.  
Because the \delta _i are independent,

  E[ \prod _{i=1}^{d} \delta _i ] = \prod _{i=1}^{d} E[ \delta _i ]  
                      = \prod _{i=1}^{d} ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i).          (6)

Step 5.  Mean value over one complete B-block.  
Combining (2) and (6),

  (1/B) \sum _{q=1}^{B} F(q) = \prod _{i=1}^{d} ( b_i^2 - 1 )/(4 b_i^2).    (7)

Step 6.  Cesaro mean over an arbitrary long block post-B.  
Because of the periodicity (3),

  \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) = \sum _{q=1}^{n} F(q).                        (8)

Write n = K B + r with 0 \leq  r < B.  Split the sum in (8) into the first K complete periods plus a tail of length r:

  \sum _{q=1}^{n} F(q)  
  = K \sum _{q=1}^{B} F(q) + \sum _{q=KB+1}^{KB+r} F(q).                  (9)

The first term equals K B\cdot \prod _{i}(b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2) by (7).
For the tail we use |\delta _i(q)| \leq  (b_i-1)/2, so, from (2),

  0 \leq  F(q) \leq  \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i-1)/(2 b_i) := M.                   (10)

Therefore the tail is < M B  (because r < B).  Putting everything together and dividing by n+1 = K B + r + 1,

  |  (1/(n+1)) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) - \prod _{i}(b_i^2-1)/(4 b_i^2) |
      \leq   (M B + \prod _{i}(b_i^2-1)/(4 b_i^2)\cdot (B-1)) / (n+1)            (11)

which is O(1/(n+1)).  Letting n \to  \infty  forces the right-hand side to 0, yielding

  lim_{n\to \infty }  1/(n+1) \sum _{q=B+1}^{B+n} F(q) = \prod _{i=1}^{d} (b_i^2 - 1)/(4 b_i^2). \square
